coredata.dc:title: Enhanced luminescent properties of carbon quantum dots on thermo-responsive vinylcaprolactam hydrogel matrix
coredata.dc:creator: Villegas, Emilia
Diaz-Barrios, Antonio
González, Gema
Chimborazo, Johnny
Reinoso, Carlos
Caetano, Manuel
de Lima, Lola
Gardener, Jules
Solorzano, Guillermo
coredata.dc:description: This research consistsof the preparation and luminescence assessment of hybrid materials made by mixing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) obtained, by a hydrothermal process, from avocado seeds, and vinyl caprolactam (VCL) based thermo-responsive hydrogels. These hydrogels were made by aqueous emulsion homo polymerization of VCL or by the copolymerization of VCL with polyethylene glycol diacrylate (PEGDA), as crosslinking agent. The hybrid materials, hydrogels and CQDs were characterized by FTIR, Raman and XPS spectroscopies and to determine the particle sizes of the different obtained materials either High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) or Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) were used. It was found that the combination of the VCL based hydrogels with carbon dots causes an enhancement on the photoluminescence when compared with the bare CQDs. Moreover, an increase in the crosslinking degree of the hydrogel polymeric gel corresponded to more enhancement in the photoluminescence intensity of the hybrid materials. The aforementioned luminescent performance and the biocompatible properties of its components position these materials as potential biomedical thermo-responsive sensors
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Luminescence
Hydrogels
Spectroscopy
Quantum confinement

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of Poly(VCL-co-PEGDA) synthesized hydrogels.
FTIR spectrum of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs).
HRTEM of Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). a) Dark Field image, b) HRTEM image of CQDs.
Raman spectra of hydrogel 23–000, CQDs, and hybrid material 23–000.
TGA thermograms of hydrogels and hybrid materials.

XPS of the core level of C1 s, N1 s from the high cross-linked panels a, d and g compared with the CQD spectra (panels b, e, h). Panel c, f and i present the levels of the lower cross-linked Hybrid material.

The normalized UV–vis (red line) and photoluminescence (blue line) spectra of the CQDs. Inset are pictures of the colloidal CQDs dispersion at room illumination (left) and when irradiated with UV light of 365 nm (right).

The luminescence spectra of hydrogels (grey solid line), CQDs (dashed line) and hybrid materials (solid upper line) samples at 25 °C for: (a) Hybrid 23–000, hydrogel 23–000 and the CQDs (b) Hybrid 23–002, hydrogel 23–002 and the CQDs (c) Hybrid 23–004, hydrogel 23–004 and the CQDs (c) Hybrid 23–000, 23–002 and 23–004 and the CQDs (dashed line).

Effects of temperature on the PL spectra of: (a) The CQDs (b), Hybrid Material 23–000 (c) Hybrid material 23–003 (d) Hybrid material 23–004. Insets: The PL intensity of composites between 22 and 45 °C.
Fluorescence spectrum and the fitting result for a solution of Carbon Dots (20 µ g/mL).

Integrated fluorescence intensity plotted against 1–10-
A for each material.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Reaction conditions: rpm=350; t = 6–7 h; T = 70C.
The concentration of VCL was 2.0 wt%.
The concentration of initiator was 0.7 wt% of VCL.
The concentration of buffer and emulsifier were 1.7 wt% of VCL.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR assignments for CQDs and avocado powder precursor Wavenumber (cm−1).
Mean particle size and PDI of VCL-co-PEGDA hydrogels and hybrid CQD- Hydrogel at RT.
Peak positions and band assigned vibration modes in the Raman spectra of the hydrogels, CQD and hybrid materials.
Temperature of maximum degradation for hydrogel and hybrid materials.
Photoluminescence maximum peak position for hybrid materials and the CQDs.

head.ce:title: Enhanced luminescent properties of carbon quantum dots on thermo-responsive vinylcaprolactam hydrogel matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences and Engineering, Yachay Tech University, Urcuquí 100119, Ecuador

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Sciences and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemical Sciences and Engineering, Yachay Tech University, Urcuquí 100119, Ecuador

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yachay Tech University, School of Physical Sciences and Nanotechnology, Urcuqui, 100119, Ecuador

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Sciences and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bYachay Tech University, School of Physical Sciences and Nanotechnology, Urcuqui, 100119, Ecuador

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Harvard University, School of Engineering and Applied Technology, Allston, 02134, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Engineering and Applied Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cHarvard University, School of Engineering and Applied Technology, Allston, 02134, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research consistsof the preparation and luminescence assessment of hybrid materials made by mixing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) obtained, by a hydrothermal process, from avocado seeds, and vinyl caprolactam (VCL) based thermo-responsive hydrogels. These hydrogels were made by aqueous emulsion homo polymerization of VCL or by the copolymerization of VCL with polyethylene glycol diacrylate (PEGDA), as crosslinking agent. The hybrid materials, hydrogels and CQDs were characterized by FTIR, Raman and XPS spectroscopies and to determine the particle sizes of the different obtained materials either High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) or Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) were used. It was found that the combination of the VCL based hydrogels with carbon dots causes an enhancement on the photoluminescence when compared with the bare CQDs. Moreover, an increase in the crosslinking degree of the hydrogel polymeric gel corresponded to more enhancement in the photoluminescence intensity of the hybrid materials. The aforementioned luminescent performance and the biocompatible properties of its components position these materials as potential biomedical thermo-responsive sensors

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels are highly versatile materials widely used in biomedical and environmental applications due to their ability to absorb water and respond to external stimuli. Among the various types, thermo-responsive hydrogels have gained significant attention because of their capacity to change their physical state in response to temperature variations. Poly(N-vinylcaprolactam) (PVCL)-based hydrogels offer unique properties such as biocompatibility, low toxicity, and temperature-sensitive behavior, making them ideal for applications ranging from drug delivery to tissue engineering [1–4].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon-based nanoparticles, classified into subgroups depending on their morphology and crystallinity. They exhibit adjustable physical, chemical, and optical properties, which can be easily tailored through synthesis methods. Moreover, CQDs are known for their non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and responsiveness to chemical and physical stimuli. They are generally classified into three types: graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CQDs) and carbonized polymeric dots (CPQDs). Most carbon dots consist of a core of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon with surface functional groups [5–7]. Their properties are determined by their chemical composition but are significantly influenced by the synthesis conditions. Optimizing conditions for the synthesis of CQDs with Photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) has become a challenging task [8–10].

Not only by changing the synthesis route of the CQDs is it possible to modulate its properties, but by changing the carbon resource structure is also possible [8–11]. In this context, through a solvothermal reaction, Tan et al. synthesized GQDs presenting white luminescence with a PLQY of 28.68 %, highlighting the versatility of the quantum dots (QDs) optical properties. This versatility extends their potential applications beyond those already mentioned (sensors and bioimaging), including their use white light emitting diodes (WLED) [12].

Moreover, following the same pathway of solvothermal synthesis, GQDs showing green- and red-emission bands were synthesized from 2,7-dihydroxynaphthalene (2,7-DHN) and piperazine anhydrous (PA) as raw materials. Due to their strong photobleaching resistance, minimal toxicity, and high-water solubility, these GQDs proved to be effective biomarkers for fluorescent bioimaging, as demonstrated by their use in labeling HeLa cells in vitro [13].

The optical properties of CQDs are mainly governed by the size of their π-conjugated structural domains and the characteristics of their surface and edges and the size-dependent quantum confinement effect [14].

In recent years, carbon quantum dots derived from organic waste materials like avocado seeds [5,7,15] have emerged as promising nanomaterials due to their excellent luminescence properties, low toxicity, and environmental sustainability. Different types of biomass-based waste material have been used: agricultural residues, plant leaves, wood, charcoal, coal); and industrial waste [16–19]. Moreover, the synthesis from natural products could result in the attachment of heteroatoms containing moieties into the surface increasing the luminescence emission

These nanodots can be easily incorporated into hydrogels, forming hybrid systems with enhanced optical, mechanical, and chemical properties. The exceptional photophysical properties and long-term colloidal stability of these systems, has opened exciting new avenues in materials science. Incorporating CQDs into polymeric hydrogel nanocomposites has led to the development of innovative materials that synergize the distinct features of both components. The integration of CQDs into PVCL-based hydrogels offers the potential for developing multifunctional materials with applications in biosensing, imaging, and controlled drug delivery, particularly in thermo-responsive systems [20–22]. Different studies on carbon dots embedded in hydrogels have been reported for drug delivery [23–25]. Roy [23] studied carbon quantum dots (CQDs) deposited on poly (N-vinyl caprolactam) grafted with β-cyclodextrin (β-CD), followed by cross-linking of diethylene glycol dimethyl acrylate, showing nontoxic and fluorescent property which helped to track the cells in vitro.

The present study presents the synthesis and characterization of a thermo-responsive PVCL-based hydrogel loaded with carbon quantum dots derived from avocado seeds. The study investigates the physicochemical properties of hydrogel, including its temperature-triggered response and luminescence behavior with potential for bioimaging and other biomedical applications. By utilizing avocado seeds as a carbon source, this work also highlights a sustainable approach to low-cost nanomaterial synthesis, from an abundant availability natural resource, contributing to the development of eco-friendly and functional materials for a wide range of applications.

For the synthesis of the thermo-responsive hydrogels the reagents used were: N-vinylcaprolactam (VCL; Sigma Aldrich, 98 %), Poly (ethylene glycol) diacrylate (PEGDA; Sigma Aldrich, Mn 250), ammonium persulfate (APS; FMC Corporation, 99 %), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS STEOL® CS-230 Stepan), sodium hydrogen carbonate (Sigma Aldrich, 99.7 %). Regarding to the synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dots, Ecuadorian avocado seeds powder served as the primary raw material, and absolute ethanol (EMSURE® Merck Millipore) was used during the process.

The synthesis was carried out for 3 thermo-responsive hydrogels: poly (vinyl caprolactam) (PVCL) and two copolymers of vinyl caprolactam (VCL) and Poly (ethylene glycol) diacrylate (PEGDA) obtained via emulsion polymerization, in a round bottom flask under reflux conditions and continuous stirring. In this case PEGDA as a crosslinker, SDS as an emulsifier, APS as an initiator, and sodium hydrogen carbonate as a buffer. All synthesized hydrogels share a constant concentration of VCL, with PEGDA concentrations ranging from 2 to 4 wt. %, based on VCL monomer (BOM). The synthesis procedures for all three were conducted under identical conditions.

Avocado seeds were selected as the carbon source. The seeds were collected and allowed to dry under sunlight for 15 days until all moisture was eliminated. Once thoroughly dried, the seeds were manually cut into small pieces and then further transformed into powder using a ball mill, at a frequency of 35 Hz for 1 hour. After grinding, the obtained powder was left to dry for 24 h in the stove set at 70 °C. Subsequently, upon removal from the oven, the powder was sieved and dissolved into 50 mL of ethanol. This solution was homogenized for one hour at 12,700 rpm. Once the organic precursor has been processed, the synthesis of CQDs was performed by hydrothermal synthesis. Initially, the reactor chamber is loaded with 2 g of the precursor material and 10 mL of distilled water is added and placed in the stove overnight at 200 °C. Then, 0.5 g of the obtained material were dispersed in 10 mL of distilled water using an ultrasound tip at intervals of 5 mins for 15 min. The samples were centrifuged for 30 mins at 10,000 revolutions. Once all the CDs samples were obtained, they were kept stored in the refrigerator at approximately 5 °C.

The hybrid Quantum Dots-Hydrogels were prepared by combining both components at a 1:1 ratio of volumes. The mixing process was executed using an ultrasound tip with an amplitude set at 50 %, applying pulses of 5.0 s each 1.0 s, spanning a total duration of 5 mins.
The solutions of quinine sulphate standard in the range 4.0 × 10–7 to 2.0 × 10–6 M were prepared in sulfuric acid 0.1 N as solvent.

The structural characterization of the materials was carried out using a UV/VIS spectrophotometer (SPECORD S600 from Analytik Jena) and the Raman spectra were recorded using a LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer from HORIBA at 633 nm. Freeze- dried samples of the hydrogels were also analyzed using FT-IR, Agilent Cary 630 FTIR spectrometer FT-IR (Diamond ATR) in the range of 4000–600 cm−1, with a spectral resolution of 2cm−1. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed with a TGA 550 from TA Instruments, employing a heating rate of 10 °C per minute from 20 °C to 600 °C in air. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) of all the materials was measured using a NanoBrook 90Plus particle size analyzer. X-Ray photoelectric spectroscopy was carried out by PHI 5000 Versaprobe III, using 250 kV and 50 kV for Survey exploration and high-resolution measurements correspondently. The system is equipped with an Al k-alpha X-Ray quartz monochromated source of 1486.4 eV, calibrated by fermi edge [26]. The quantum dots were observed by HR-TEM using a Field Emission TEM Jeol F200 operating at 200kV.

Additionally, the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired using a QE Pro High-Performance Spectrometer from Ocean Insights. Luminescence excitation was conducted using an LSM LED connected to a controller unit, with the selected laser for the measurements operating at 365 nm. Photoluminescence measurements were carried out for all the samples with an integration time of 100 ms, with 3 scans to average and a boxcar width of 5. The measurements were also conducted at different temperature values within the range of 4 to 42 °C. The UV–Vis spectra were obtained in a range from 200 nm to 1100 nm using a diode array ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer, Specord S600 from Analytikjena and 1 cm path length quartz cells. To prevent inner filter effects or reabsorption of the emitted fluorescence radiation, the sample solutions were diluted so that the sample absorbance was <0.01. Steady state fluorescence spectra were collected in a range of 200 nm to 775 nm using a QEPro UV–Vis spectrometer from Ocean Optics, equipped with a 365 nm LED excitation source and a CCD detector, and 1 cm path length quartz cells. The spectra of both quinine sulfate and quantum dots solutions were recorded under the same conditions: (Slit: 10 µm, optical resolution: 1.2 nm, integration time: 10 ms, SN: 1000:1).

The commonly accepted definition of quantum yield (
ϕ
) is the number of a photons emitted per photon absorbed by the system [27]
(1)
ϕ =
n u m b e r
o f
p h o t o n s
e m i t t e d
n u m b e r
o f
p h o t o n s
a b s o r b e d
and can be measured optically, using either the relative or absolute method. The relative quantum yield,
ϕ S
, of a sample can be calculated using [28]:
(2)
ϕ S
=
ϕ R
(
I S
I R
)
(
1 −
10
−
A R
1 −
10
−
A S
)
(
η S
η R
)
2
where the subscripts S and R denote the sample and reference respectively.
ϕ R
i is the quantum yield of the reference standard, I is the integrated fluorescence spectrum intensity, A is the absorbance of the solution at the excitation wavelength (
λ
e x
) and
η
is the refractive index of the solution solvent.

If a series of solutions of different concentrations is used, Eq. (2) can be expressed as a linear equation for calculating the quantum yield [29]
(3)
ϕ S
=
ϕ R
(
I S
I R
)
(
1 −

−
A R
1 −

−
A S
)

)
2
(4)
ϕ S
=
ϕ R
(
G r a
d S
G r a
d R
)
(
η S
η R
)
2

By plotting
I S
versus
1 −
10
−
A S
and
1 −
10
−
A R
versus
I R
, the "gradients" (slopes) can be obtained and used in eq (3) to obtain
ϕ S
. This method can identify potential systematic errors that would appear at higher concentrations. 0.1 M quinine sulfate solutions in H2SO4 were used as a standard for relative quantum yield measurements.

The photostability data was acquired by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs and their hybrids under continuous illumination over time. The fluorescence intensity at each time point (F) was normalized against the initial intensity at time zero (Fo). This normalization allowed us to effectively compare the relative photostability of the samples without interference from variations in absolute intensity. The experiments were conducted under identical illumination conditions to ensure a fair comparison.
In Table 1 the polymerization conditions and the corresponding polymer yield of the obtained polymers are summarized

The FTIR spectra for the 3 different hydrogels are shown in Fig. 1 . Absorption bands are observed at 1612 cm−1, 3417, and 2928 cm−1 for the homopolymer PNVCL and for the 2 copolymers; these bands were assigned as the stretching vibrations of the CO group in the amide functional group, OH functional group, and the C–H functional group, respectively. The three spectra exhibit the lack of CC stretching, CH stretching, and CC out-of-plane bending compared to the monomer VCL spectrum. This absence confirms that polymerization has occurred. The strong stretching band of the VCL amide group's CO is observed at approximately 1612 cm−1, while the CN stretching occurs around 1479 cm−1 in all the hydrogels. Both peaks exhibited a shift towards lower frequencies in the polymers compared to the monomer VCL. The CO (ester) stretching band at 1720 cm−1 in PEGDA and its displacement to a higher frequency in the hydrogels, suggests the absence of conjugation.

In Fig. 2, the characteristic broad band of the stretching vibration of -OH groups at 3250 cm−1 for CQDs and avocado powder are observed. Additionally, the signal for aliphatic CH vibrations is present in both spectra. Furthermore, a new absorption in the CQDs is noted in 1704 and 1592 cm−1, which could correspond to carbonyl groups and CC bonds, respectively. The shift of both peaks to lower frequencies that are observed may be attributed to the conjugation of CO with α, β CC bonds. The peak observed in CQDs at 1405 cm−1 is assigned to CH bend vibration [30]. In CQDs spectrum, the peak appearing at 1021 cm−1 is also notable, which is assigned to CO stretching vibrations. Finally, at 951 cm−1 is a small sharp peak that could correspond to the formation of epoxy groups during the synthesis procedure [30].

The CQDs show high crystallinity with an average size of 7.5 nm as can be observed in the dark field image (Fig. 3 a). The HRTEM image (Fig. 3b) shows distinct lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 2.5 nm corresponding to the (1 0 0) plane characteristic of graphene. Similar morphology of carbon dots has been reported obtained from green materials.

Fig. 4 displays the FTIR spectra for the hybrid materials under study. The wavenumber values closely resemble those of the hydrogels, indicating a high degree of similarity. The signals associated with CQDs are overshadowed by the presence of VCL-co-PEGDA hydrogels. It is noticeable that the CO carbonyl band at 1720 cm−1 for hydrogels 23–000, 23–002 and 23–004 in Fig. 1 remain at the same position for Hybrids 23–000, 23–002 and 23–004 in Fig. 4.

As shown in Table 3 , when comparing Hydrogel 23–000, which has no crosslinking agent, to Hydrogel 23–002 and Hydrogel 23–004, cross-linked with different concentrations of PEGDA, an increase on the average particle size of hydrogels is observed. This might be attributed to an increase solvation of the polymer due to hydrophilicity of the PEGDA. The same effect is observed in corresponding hybrid material.

The Raman spectra is shown in Fig. 5 of the hydrogel 23–000, the CQDs and the hybrid material 23–000 and the assigned vibrations are presented in Table 4 . In the spectrum of the hydrogel 23–000, the vibrations of the principal functional groups present in the structure of hydrogels, are at 703, 1446, 1623 and 2857–2930 cm−1 corresponding to the δNCO, δ- CH2, δ-CO and ν-CH2, respectively, in good agreement with Sanna et al. [31]. On the other hand, for the CQDs it is possible to elucidate 6 principal signals. The vibrations at 1442 and 2853–2920 cm−1, are assigned to the asymmetric and symmetric vibration modes of the -CH2 groups present in the surface of the CQDs. The signals at 1262, 1300 and 1656 cm−1 matched with the characteristic vibrations of epoxy, CO and CC groups. The latter gives the carbon dots the sp2 character that is characteristic of carbonaceous nanomaterials.

In the hybrid material 23–000 spectra, only the signals from the Poly (VCL- co-PEGDA) hydrogels are detectable. The signals corresponding to the carbon dots are disguised by hydrogel signals. Only the most significant signals at 705, 1444, and 2860- 2932 cm−1 can be appreciated, corresponding to the vibrational modes δ-N-CO, δ-CH2, and ν-CH2, respectively.

The TGA thermograms of hydrogels and characteristic hybrid material are presented in Fig. 6 , all groups of materials presented good thermal stability and exhibit a large weight loss at approximately 400 °C associated with the degradation of the VCL-co-PEGDA hydrogels, as appreciated in Table 5 .

The XPS analysis of the samples with the lowest and highest degrees of crosslinking shows clear evidence of new chemical bonds forming between the polymer matrix and the carbon quantum dots (CQDs). In this analysis, both survey and high-resolution spectra were examined for the main detected elements.

The C 1 s spectrum shows a peak at 284.5 eV, wich aligns with the C–C bonds as expected to find in the ring structure. Moving to higher energies, it can be seen a peak at 285.3 eV that indicates the presence of C–N bonds. Additional peaks at 286 eV and 288 eV correspond to C–O and CO bonds, typical of the organic polymer components [32]. The CC at 284.5 is present in a larger quantity in the CQDs deconvolution in agreement with its sp2 nature binding configuration as is presented in panel b) of Fig. 7 . This contributes to the deconvoluted carbon level shown in panel c) of Fig. 6 of the hybrid structure. When at the N 1 s spectrum Fig. 7 panels (d) to (i) are observed, the most prominent feature is a peak at 398.4 eV, which confirms the presence of N–CO bonds. These bonds are characteristic of the heterocyclic rings in the CQDs. This signal is also seen in the hydrogel and hybrid materials, alongside the N–CH signal, which points to nitrogen-containing groups [33–35] Since the nitrogen content in the CQDs is quite low, this signal fivefold amplified, as shown in Supplementary Figure S1. Notably, the concentration of N–H bonds vary, with the lower crosslinked hydrogel (Fig. 7g) showing higher levels of N–H than the more highly crosslinked hydrogel (Fig. 7d). Interestingly, the hybrid material seems largely unaffected by nitrogen contributions from the CQDs, as seen in Fig. 7, panels f and i. As for oxygen, it shows up at binding energies above 531 eV, which confirms its role in the organic structure of the polymer. In Fig. 7 XPS of the core level of C1 s, N1 s from the high cross-linked panels a, d and g compared with the CQD spectra (panels b, e, h). Panel c, f and i present the levels of the lower cross-linked Hybrid material.

[36,37]. However, the O1 s core level of CQDs present in its deconvolution peak at 527 eV, 530 Ev and 531.3 eV confirming the presence of Oxygen in metallic oxides and some carbonates according to its complex reagents from avocado as is seen in (Supplementary Figure S2)9999. Again, the nitrogen signal from the CQDs was amplified due to their low nitrogen content, which canbe also observed in the survey spectrum (Supplementary Figure S1)

Fig. 8 shows the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the CQDs aqueous solution (red), revealing two prominent absorption peaks. The first absorption region at 193.5 nm attributed to the π-π∗ transition. This peak corresponds to conjugated double bonds, which in the case of the synthesized CQDs would correspond to the CC bonds that was identified by both FTIR and Raman spectroscopy. These electronic transitions involve the excitation of an electron from a π molecular orbital (double bond) to a π∗ orbital (antibonding). There is also a less intense peak at 278 nm associated with the n–π∗ transition. Fig. 8 also displays an emission band at 463 nm corresponding to the photoluminescence of the CQDs aqueous colloidal dispersion when exposed to UV light of 365 nm.

The luminescence spectra for the hybrids prepared from hydrogels with different degrees of crosslinking is seen in Fig. 9 , in relation to the bare colloidal CQDs dispersions. Figs. 9a, 9b and 9c show that the hybrids material has a remarkable higher intensity than the corresponding hydrogels and CQDs from which they were made, evidencing a synergistic luminescent effect between the CQDs and the hydrogel. On the other side, Fig. 9d, indicates that the degree of crosslinking from 2 to 4 % (base on monomer) enhance the luminescence of the resulting hybrid materials, which might be attributed to a relative increase in rigidity when degree of crosslinking goes go from 2 to 4 % BOM.

It's also worth noting that the wavelength at which the maximum luminescence intensity occurs for all hybrid shifts towards lower wavelengths 456, 452 and 453 nm (higher energies) for hybrids 23–000, 23–002 and 23–004, respectively, in relation to the maximum intensity for the corresponding CQDs with maximum peak at 463 nm. This can be appreciated in the plots in Fig. 9 and tabulated in Table 6 . This is attributed to the interaction of the CQDs with hydrogels that might be responsible of the enhancement of luminescence of the corresponding hybrid materials, this is in good agreement with other reports [38]

In addition, luminescence analyses were conducted to explore the effect of thermal responsiveness of these hybrid materials. Fig. 10 a shows the CQDs colloidal dispersion experienced a slight increase in luminescence as the temperature decreased. Moreover, Figs. 10b, 10c and 10d show noticeable increase in luminescence when temperature is decreased (from 45 to 22 °C). This behavior can be attributed to the lower mobility, with decreasing temperature, of the CQDs entrapped on the hydrogel's matrix, also known as quantum-size confinement effect [39].

VCL based gels experience lower critical solution temperature (LCST) at approximately 32 °C [1], thus at temperatures below 32 °C, the hydrogel forms a homogeneous network where the CQDs are evenly distributed in transparent gel matrix exhibiting strong luminescence emission. At higher temperatures than LCST, the VCL based hydrogels tend to form a heterogeneous network since they induce phase separation. This phase separation leads to turbidity as collapsed hydrogel particles serve as strong scattering centers, reducing luminescence for hybrid materials [39].

The nature of bonding plays a crucial role in affecting this variation in luminescence intensity corresponding to changes in temperature. When CQDs are entrapped or diffused within hydrogels without covalent bonds, for instance through hydrophobic forces, hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions; there is an observable decrease in luminescence intensity as temperatures increased [30,40]. In this study observations about LCST behavior, explain why the rise in temperatures led to decrease on luminescence intensity, confirming that interaction between the synthesized carbon dots and polymer matrix do not involve covalent bonds but likely involved hydrogen bonds formed between the nitrogen-containing functional groups present in the surface of the CQDs and the carbonyl groups within VCL-co-PEGDA matrix hydrogels, as shown by XPS analysis.

The resulting spectra were processed using the Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm for iterative nonlinear curve fitting to adjust a log-normal four-parametric function (a skewed Gaussian equation) to the data. [41,42]
(5)
F
( λ )
=
I m
exp
{
(
−
ln 2
l
n
2
ρ
)
ln
2
(
(
1 a
−
1 λ
)
(
1 a
−
1
λ m
)
)
}
The fitted functions were integrated into the 300–1100 nm range with the values for the parameters determined from the fit to obtain the integrated fluorescence intensity.

The obtained data are displayed in Fig. 12 , showing the integrated fluorescence intensity plotted against 1–10-
A for each material. Linear fits were applied to both datasets to determine the gradients, calculated as CQDs-Grad = 9.37 and Ref-Grad = 78.72 for the carbon dots and quinone sulfate solutions, respectively.

The quantum yield of quinone sulfate solutions was taken from literature to be
ϕ R
= 54.6 % and the refractive indexes of water and sulfuric acid 0.1 M are
η
w a t e r
= 1.33, and
η
S u l f u r i c
= 1.33 respectively. Using Eq (4), the quantum yield of the carbon dots solutions was calculated to be
ϕ
C D
= 6.50 %. This relatively low quantum yield is consistent with literature reports for non-doped CQDs synthesized under similar conditions and further supports the validity of our experimental approach [15,43,44].

As seen in Fig. 13 the CQDs and their hybrids do not exhibit significant differences in photostability. Specifically, all the systems retained > 90 % of their initial fluorescence intensity after 65 h of continuous exposure. A slight decrease below 90 % was observed at 71 h, suggesting excellent stability under prolonged illumination. These findings demonstrate that incorporating the gels into the hybrids does not significantly impact the photostability of the CQDs. The nearly equivalent photostability profiles suggest that the gels in the hybrids serve as a matrix without significantly altering the intrinsic photophysical properties of the CQDs. This observation underscores the robustness of the CQDs themselves and the compatibility of the gels as host matrices.

The presence of PVCL and VCL-co-PEGDA hydrogels has a synergistic effect on the luminescent properties of the prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs) species. The hydrogel crosslinking degree has a slight improvement in photoluminescence. This might be due to a relative increase in the rigidity of the hydrogel structure.

The interaction between the CQDs and hydrogels enhances the stability, dispersion and luminescence of hybrid materials. Furthermore, the quantum confinement effect plays an important role in the optical properties of the obtained hybrid materials, giving them potential for sensor applications.

These results are significant for potential applications of the CQDs and their hybrids in long-term photonic and bioimaging applications. The ability to maintain fluorescence intensity over extended illumination periods is a key attribute for such uses.

The calculated quantum yield
ϕ S
/
ϕ R
for the synthesized CQDs was determined to be 0.065. This relatively low quantum yield is consistent with literature reports for non-doped CQDs synthesized under similar conditions and further supports the validity of our experimental approach.

The consistency of the
ϕ S
/
ϕ R
values across measurements demonstrates the robustness of the methodology.

Emilia Villegas: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Antonio Diaz-Barrios: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Gema González: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Johnny Chimborazo: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Carlos Reinoso: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Data curation. Manuel Caetano: Formal analysis, Data curation. Lola de Lima: Investigation, Formal analysis. Jules Gardener: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Guillermo Solorzano: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
